coredata.dc:title: A sensitive multicolor fluorescence sensing strategy for chlorotetracycline based on bovine serum albumin-stabilized copper nanocluster
coredata.dc:creator: Bi, Ning
Xi, Ya-Hua
Hu, Mei-Hua
Xu, Jun
Gou, Jian
Li, Yong-Xin
Zhang, Li-Na
Jia, Lei
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent probes with on-site visual detection function have received extensive attention in the detection of chlortetracycline (CTC), which was widely used in aquaculture and animal husbandry. Copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) with excellent optical properties were prepared using bovine serum albumin (BSA) as a template, and a multicolor fluorescence strategy based on BSA-stabilized Cu NCs (BSA-Cu NCs) for detecting CTC was proposed. BSA-Cu NCs had a red emission at 640 nm. After the addition of CTC, the red emission of BSA-Cu NCs gradually decreased for internal filtering effect, while the green emission of CTC was significantly enhanced under the sensitization of BSA. This simple sensing process can be achieved in real time by directly mixing the target sample with BSA-Cu NCs, and the detection limit (LOD) of the system for CTC was 12.01 nM. Based on this sensing strategy, a fluorescence film sensing detection platform was constructed to achieve ultra-fast detection of CTC within 30 s. This work provided a fluorescent film sensor with the advantages of portability, ultra-fast and low cost, which provided a feasible alternative for on-site ultra-fast screening of CTC.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multicolor fluorescence
Copper nanocluster
Chlorotetracycline
Fluorescent film sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of red emission BSA-Cu NCs
Detection of CTC by BSA-Cu NCs multicolor fluorescence nanoprobe
The application of BSA-Cu NCs multicolor fluorescence nanoprobe in actual samples
Microstructure characterization of BSA-Cu NCs nanoprobe
Optimization of detection conditions for CTC by BSA-Cu NCs multicolor fluorescence nanoprobe
BSA-Cu NCs multicolor fluorescence nanoprobe detection of CTC
Detection Mechanism of CTC by BSA-Cu NCs nanoprobe
Selectivity and anti-interference of BSA-Cu NCs nanoprobe detection for CTC
Visual real-time detection of CTC using fluorescent film and smartphone APP

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and (b) HRTEM image of BSA-Cu NCs, (c) The energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) spectroscopy of BSA-Cu NCs, (d) XRD patterns of BSA-Cu NCs.
(a) The FTIR spectra of BSA and BSA-Cu NCs, (b) XPS full scanning spectrum of BSA-Cu NCs, (c) High resolution spectra of C 1 s, (d) O 1 s, (e) N 1 s and (f) Cu 2p.

(a) Excitation spectra of BSA-Cu NCs (Illustration was the corresponding excitation fluorescence intensity ratio I535/I635), (b) The influence on fluorescence intensity ratio of I535/I635 in different buffer solutions, (c) The effect of incubation time on fluorescence intensity ratio of I535/I635, (d) Fluorescence test of BSA-Cu NCs within 30 days (CCTC=5 μM).

(a) and (b) Fluorescence spectra of BSA-Cu NCs with CTC concentration in the range of 0–6.5 μM and 0–70 μM, respectively, (λex=365 nm), (c) Linear relationship between I535/I635 with CTC concentration in the range of 0–40 μM, (d) Fluorescence photos of BSA-Cu NCs nanoprobe with different concentrations of TC.
(a) The excitation, emission spectrum and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of BSA-Cu NCs, (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum of BSA-Cu NCs, CTC, and BSA-Cu NCs + CTC.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of BSA-Cu NCs with other antibiotics, (b) Fluorescence images of BSA-Cu NCs with other antibiotics under 365 nm UV lamp, (c) The effect of interfering substances on the fluorescence intensity ratio of I535/I635 of BSA-Cu NCs in the presence and absence of CTC, (c) Fluorescence images of BSA-Cu NCs with interfering substances under 365 nm UV lamp. The concentration of CTC, interfering substances and other antibiotics were 5 μM.
Visualization detection of CTC by combined PVA fluorescent film based on BSA-Cu NCs nanoprobe with smartphone color scanning APP.
Synthesis of BSA-Cu NCs multicolor fluorescence nanoprobe and visual detection of CTC.
Comparison of different fluorimetric methods for CTC detection.
The detection results of CTC in real samples of BSA-Cu NCs.

head.ce:title: A sensitive multicolor fluorescence sensing strategy for chlorotetracycline based on bovine serum albumin-stabilized copper nanocluster

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo 454000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo 454000, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo 454000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo 454000, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent probes with on-site visual detection function have received extensive attention in the detection of chlortetracycline (CTC), which was widely used in aquaculture and animal husbandry. Copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) with excellent optical properties were prepared using bovine serum albumin (BSA) as a template, and a multicolor fluorescence strategy based on BSA-stabilized Cu NCs (BSA-Cu NCs) for detecting CTC was proposed. BSA-Cu NCs had a red emission at 640 nm. After the addition of CTC, the red emission of BSA-Cu NCs gradually decreased for internal filtering effect, while the green emission of CTC was significantly enhanced under the sensitization of BSA. This simple sensing process can be achieved in real time by directly mixing the target sample with BSA-Cu NCs, and the detection limit (LOD) of the system for CTC was 12.01 nM. Based on this sensing strategy, a fluorescence film sensing detection platform was constructed to achieve ultra-fast detection of CTC within 30 s. This work provided a fluorescent film sensor with the advantages of portability, ultra-fast and low cost, which provided a feasible alternative for on-site ultra-fast screening of CTC.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chlortetracycline (CTC) is a broad antibiotic drug with high antibacterial activity and low price, which is used to treat microbial infections of animals and plants in animal husbandry and promote the growth of livestock [1,2]. However, animals cannot fully absorb CTC, and some of its residues enter the ecosystem through feces or surface water, ultimately affecting human life and health, which is a very difficult problem for public health [3,4]. In recent years, a large number of CTC residues have been detected in milk, meat and honey. The abuse of CTC leaded to adverse physiological reactions such as allergic reactions, which seriously threatened human health. Therefore, it is urgent to establish a high sensitivity CTC detection method. The detection methods of CTC mainly include electrochemical method [5], microbial method [6], ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy [7], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [8] and fluorometry [9]. Among them, fluorometry did not require complex sample processing and expensive equipment, and had outstanding characteristics such as sensitivity, high efficiency and simple operation, which had attracted extensive attention.

However, traditional fluorescent probes exist some defects due to relying on a single fluorescence response signal. Compared with the single-emission fluorescent probe, the dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe has the self-calibration function of correcting the signal, which can effectively eliminate the detection error caused by background interference and various environmental factors [10–13]. At present, several ratiometric fluorescent probes for CTC detection have been developed. For example, Li et al. embedded CdTe quantum dots (QDs) into ZIF-8 to form a ratiometric fluorescent probe for CTC detection [14]. Lai et al. synthesized a novel dual-emission carbon dots by one-step synthesis for high selectivity and high sensitivity recognition of CTC [15]. In addition, the ratiometric fluorescent probes that combined more than two fluorophores (dyes, carbon dots and rare earth ions (Eu3+)) into a hybrid by chelation was proposed for the analysis of CTC [16–18].

In recent years, metal nanoclusters (M NCs) have shown great potential in the field of fluorescent nanoprobes and bioimaging due to their advantages of ultra-small size, photostability, low toxicity, biocompatibility, adjustable emission, and large Stokes shift [19]. At present, proteins, peptides and DNA was used as effective reducing agents and biological templates to synthesize M NCs. The reaction conditions for the synthesis of M NCs based on proteins were mild and do not require harsh reagents or organic solvents, and the morphology and fluorescence quantum yield can be designed by controlling the nucleation and growth of M NCs. In addition, the abundant functional groups in proteins contributed to the functionalization of fluorescent nanoclusters. Therefore, some ratiometric fluorescence methods based on M NCs as probes to detect CTC have been proposed. Zhang et al. synthesized 6-deoxy-6-mercapto-β-cyclodextrin stabilized red-emitting gold nanoclusters (6-SH-CD@Au NCs) based on the photoreduction method for the detection of CTC [20]. Li et al. used the aggregation-induced emission (AIE) characteristics of copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) to construct a ratiometric fluorescent probe (Cu NCs-Al3+) for the detection of CTC [21].

Compared with noble metal nanoclusters, the precursor resources for the preparation of Cu NCs were more abundant, cheap and easier to obtain, so that Cu NCs have received significant attention [22,23]. The synthesis of Cu NCs using bovine serum albumin (BSA) could avoid the surface oxidation of Cu NCs, and had good dispersibility and stability. In addition, due to the three-dimensional hydrophobic cavity structure inside the BSA, the analyte was specifically bound to it, thereby sensitizing the analyte to emit or enhancing its characteristic fluorescence emission [26]. Wang et al. synthesized BSA-stabilized red emission Cu NCs for the detection of rutin, and completed visual imaging detection in aqueous solution and filter paper [24]. Fan et al. synthesized BSA-stabilized blue emission Cu NCs for the detection of doxycycline (DOX) [25]. Many CTC fluorescent sensors based on Cu NCs have been proposed [27,28].

Based on the above research, BSA-stabilized red emission Cu NCs (BSA-Cu NCs) were synthesized by one-step method to construct a multicolor fluorescent nanoprobe for rapid and visual detection of CTC. Scheme 1 was the synthesis of BSA-Cu NCs and the detection process of CTC based on BSA-Cu NCs nanoprobe. BSA constructs a three-dimensional hydrophobic cavity structure on the surface of Cu NCs. CTC entered and bonded to the hydrophobic cavity without destroying the hydrophobic cavity structure, thereby enhanced the fluorescence intensity of CTC and showed its characteristic fluorescence emission peak at 535 nm. At the same time, due to the internal filtering effect, CTC inhibited the red fluorescence intensity of BSA-Cu NCs at 635 nm. Under the 365 nm UV lamp, the fluorescence color of the detection system gradually changed from red to green. Moreover, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) fluorescent film was prepared based on BSA-Cu NCs, and a fast, sensitive and real-time intelligent detection strategy of CTC was developed by combining the fluorescent film with smartphone APP, which provided new ideas for intelligent visual real-time detection of CTC.

Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was purchased from Shanghai McLin Biotechnology Co., Ltd., China. Copper sulfate (CuSO4) was purchased from Tianjin Kaixin Chemical Industry Co., Ltd., China. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was purchased from the Continental Chemical Reagent Plant in Tianjin, China. Hydrazine hydrate (N2H4⋅H2O (80%)) was derived from Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. Chlorotetracycline (CTC) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Industrial Co., Ltd., China. Other reagents used in this experiment were analytically pure and all the solution was prepared with ultrapure water. All glass containers were soaked in aqua regia overnight before used.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were recorded by Tecnai G2 F30 microscope under 300 kV accelerating voltage. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to analyze the composition of the material surface by Thermal Science ESCALAB 250 Xi. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to test the materials by standard KBr disk technology on the Bruker V70 FTIR spectrometer in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern data of the materials powder were measured by Rigaku Dmax 2400 diffractometer in the range of 2θ= 5°− 90°. The steady-state emission spectra were obtained in aqueous solution using a F97 Pro fluorescence spectrometer at an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded using Shanghai Youke T2601 UV–visible spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of red emission BSA-Cu NCs
ce:section.ce:para: BSA-Cu NCs were obtained by the synthetic steps in the published literature with slightly modifying [29]. After mixing 0.01 g BSA with 5 mL CuSO4 (5.00 ×10−3 M) aqueous solution under stirring for 5 min, the blue transparent solution gradually changed into a white hydrogel. The pH of the mixed solution was adjusted to 12 by 1 M NaOH solution, and the mixed solution gradually changed to purple transparent solution. Then 2.5 mL N2H4⋅H2O (80%) was slowly added into the above mixed solution, and the brown solution of BSA-Cu NCs was obtained after stirring for 4 h. Finally, BSA-Cu NCs were purified by 1000 Da dialysis membrane for 12 h, and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CTC by BSA-Cu NCs multicolor fluorescence nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: 300 μL BSA-Cu NCs solution was diluted to 2 mL by deionized water. The solution was titrated with different concentrations of CTC standard solution and incubated at room temperature for 12 s. The changes of fluorescence emission spectra at 535 nm and 635 nm were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 400 nm to realize the quantitative analysis of CTC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The application of BSA-Cu NCs multicolor fluorescence nanoprobe in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the actual detection effect of BSA-Cu NCs multicolor fluorescence nanoprobe, CTCs in honey, milk, lake water and BSA were analyzed, and the samples were pretreated according to the published literature [4]. The milk sample was purchased from Yonghui supermarket (Jiaozuo, China). Trichloroacetic acid (300 mg/mL) was added to the milk sample at a volume ratio of 1%, and ultrasonic treatment was performed for 20 min. Then the solution was centrifuged (5 min, 9000 rpm) to remove lipids and proteins, and further filtered using a filter membrane (0.22 µm). Finally, the pH of the solution was adjusted to 7 with 1 M NaOH. Honey was purchased from Mengshan soil honey (Linyi, China), which was diluted 40 times by ultrapure water and the large particles were removed by 0.22 µm filter membrane. The lake water was taken from Longyuan Lake (Jiaozuo, China). First, the lake water was centrifuged at 10000 rpm to remove a large amount of impurities. Then, the filter membrane (0.22 µm) was used to remove small particle impurities. The 30 mg/L BSA was sonicated and centrifuged at 9500 rpm for 5 min, and filtered through a 0.22 µm filter to remove large particles. Different concentrations of CTC standard solution were added to the treated actual samples and incubated with BSA-Cu NCs nanoprobe for 12 s to determine the fluorescence spectra.

Firstly, 5 g of PVA was dissolved in 50 mL of water and heated to 95 °C, and quickly stirred to dissolve. After cooling to room temperature, 15 mL PVA solution was mixed with 10 mL BSA-Cu NCs solution and stirred for 30 min. The above mixed solution was then dripped into the mold and dried in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h to obtain fluorescent film. Different concentrations of CTC standard solution were titrated on the fluorescent film, and the change of fluorescence color was observed under 365 nm UV lamp, and the photos were recorded by smartphone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructure characterization of BSA-Cu NCs nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of BSA-Cu NCs nanoprobe can be clearly observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. As shown in Fig. 1a and b, BSA-Cu NCs were spherical, monodispersed, and a clear lattice stripes with a lattice spacing of 0.15 nm was observed, indicating that BSA-Cu NCs had good crystallinity. Fig. 1c was the energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) of BSA-Cu NCs nanoprobe, which proved the existence of C, O, N and Cu elements, indicating the successful synthesis of BSA-Cu NCs. Fig. 1d shows the XRD pattern of BSA-Cu NCs, and a wide characteristic peak was appeared at 22.87°, which proved that BSA-stabilized Cu NCs had crystal structure [30].

The structural characteristics of BSA-Cu NCs were further analyzed by FTIR. As the FTIR spectra shown in Fig. 2a, the characteristic peak at 1391 cm−1 was originated by the bending vibration of -OH in -COOH [31]. The characteristic peaks at 1533 cm−1 and 1649 cm−1 were caused by the bending vibration of N-H and the tensile vibration of amide bond, respectively [25]. The characteristic peak at 3413 cm−1 was generated by the interaction of-OH groups, which was red shift to 3437 cm−1 of Cu NCs, also proved that BSA was successfully modified to the surface of Cu NCs. The characteristic peaks at 1533 cm−1, 1649 cm−1 and 3437 cm−1 were produced by hydrophilic groups, respectively, indicating that Cu NCs had good water solubility. In addition, by comparing the tensile peak strength of FTIR spectra between BSA-Cu NCs and BSA, it can be found that the peak tensile strength of BSA-Cu NCs was significantly reduced. This was because the disordered structure of BSA-Cu NCs increased and the helical structure decreased significantly after BSA was coated on the surface of Cu [32].

In addition, the elemental composition and chemical bond of BSA-Cu NCs were investigated by XPS. The XPS full scanning spectrum of BSA-Cu NCs (Fig. 2b) showed that the surface binding energies of BSA-Cu NCs at 286.1 eV, 532.2 eV, 399.8 eV and 942.5 eV corresponded to C 1 s, O 1 s, N 1 s and Cu 2p, respectively. As the high resolution spectra of C 1 s shown in Fig. 2c, the binding energies of 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV and 288.1 eV were corresponding to C-C, C-O and CO, respectively [33]. As shown in the O 1 s spectra (Fig. 2d), the characteristic peaks of 531.4 eV and 533.0 eV were caused by OC and C-OH/C-O-C, respectively [34]. In the high resolution spectra of N 1 s (Fig. 2e), the characteristic peak at 399.8 eV corresponded to the N-H bond [35]. As shown in Fig. 2f, the characteristic peaks of Cu 2p at 932.6 eV and 952.4 eV were corresponding to Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2, respectively, which were caused by Cu (I) and Cu (0) [36]. There was no other peak between these two characteristic peaks, indicating that there was no residual Cu (II) [37], and also indicating that BSA-Cu NCs were successfully synthesized. It can be seen from the characterization results of XPS and FTIR, there were abundant hydrophilic groups-COOH and-NH2 on the surface of BSA-Cu NCs, which improved the water solubility and dispersion of the materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of detection conditions for CTC by BSA-Cu NCs multicolor fluorescence nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the optimal detection conditions of BSA-Cu NCs multicolor fluorescent nanoprobe for CTC, the excitation wavelength, the internal environment of the detection system, and the incubation time were optimized (CCTC=5 μM). As shown in Fig. 3a, the emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths were tested in the range of 370–420 nm. When the excitation wavelength was 400 nm, the fluorescence intensity ratio of I535/I635 was the largest (the illustration in Fig. 3a), so 400 nm was selected as the best excitation wavelength. The influence of the internal environment of the detection system was shown in Fig. 3b, the response of BSA-Cu NCs on CTC in ultrapure water, Tris-HCl, HEPES, BR and PBS buffer systems (pH=7) was investigated. The fluorescence intensity ratio of I535/I635 in ultrapure water was the largest, which may be due to the internal structure of BSA was destructed under acidic or alkaline conditions, hindering the interaction between BSA and CTC. Therefore, CTC was determined in pure water system. Fig. 3c shows the effect of incubation time on CTC detection. The fluorescence intensity ratio of I535/I635 reached the maximum within 12 s and remained stable, indicating that the coordination reaction between BSA-Cu NCs and CTC was fast and stable. Finally, the fluorescence stability of BSA-Cu NCs multicolor fluorescence nanoprobe within 30 days was studied (Fig. 3d). It can be seen from Fig. 3d that the fluorescence intensity of BSA-Cu NCs did not change significantly within 30 days and maintained 94.6% of the original fluorescence intensity on the 30th day. Since the detection of CTC by BSA-Cu NCs can be completed in a short time, the multicolor fluorescent nanoprobe can meet the requirements for efficient detection of CTC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BSA-Cu NCs multicolor fluorescence nanoprobe detection of CTC
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal conditions, the detection of BSA-Cu NCs on CTC was investigated by fluorescence titration test. With the increase of CTC concentration, the red characteristic fluorescence emission intensity of BSA-Cu NCs at 635 nm gradually decreased, while a new fluorescence emission peak appeared at 535 nm, and the fluorescence intensity gradually increased ( Fig. 4a and b). The fluorescence intensity ratio of I535/I635 gradually increased with the increase of CTC concentration in the range of 0–40 μM, showing a good fitting relationship (Fig. 4c). When the CTC concentration was in the range of 0–6 μM, the linear regression equation was I535/I635 = 0.69417 C + 0.24033, the correlation coefficient R2 = 0.99016, and the detection limit (LOD) was 12.01 nM according to 3σ/slope (the illustration of Fig. 4c). The fluorescence color of the detection system gradually changed from red to green under 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 4d), and the distinct color change facilitated the visual detection of CTC.

As shown in Table 1, the multicolor fluorescence nanoprobe proposed in this study was compared with the published literature on the detection of CTC. Most of the fluorescence probes for detection of CTC were based on monochromatic fluorescence, and the sensitivity and accuracy were insufficient by forming 'Turn on' or 'Turn off' mode. In addition, the preparation process of most probes was cumbersome, and only a single fluorescence intensity was enhanced or weakened, which was not conducive to visual detection. The BSA-Cu NCs multicolor fluorescent nanoprobe proposed in this method had the advantages of simple preparation, high safety and high sensitivity. Moreover, BSA-Cu NCs multicolor fluorescent nanoprobe provided obvious fluorescence color changes from red to green in the water environment, which was convenient for visual real-time detection by the naked eye. Multicolor fluorescent probes have greatly promoted the application of visual detection in actual samples, and have great application value for the study of visual detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection Mechanism of CTC by BSA-Cu NCs nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: The intensity of the red characteristic fluorescence emission peak at 635 nm of BSA-Cu NCs was reduced in the presence of CTC, which causing the fluorescence quenching of BSA-Cu NCs (Fig. 4a). In general, the following mechanisms can cause fluorescence quenching: photoinduced electron transfer mechanism (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanism (FRET), aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) and internal filtering effect (IFE) [43]. In order to explore the quenching mechanism, the excitation spectrum, emission spectrum of BSA-Cu NCs and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CTC were tested ( Fig. 5a). The fluorescence emission peak of BSA-Cu NCs was located at 635 nm, and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CTC partially overlapped with the excitation spectrum of BSA-Cu NCs, indicating that there was no energy transfer between BSA-Cu NCs and CTC, and the quenching phenomenon was caused by the stable quenching mechanism of IFE [44].

A new fluorescence emission peak appeared at 535 nm, and the fluorescence intensity gradually increased with the increase of CTC concentration (Fig. 4a and b). As shown in Fig. 5b, the UV–vis absorption spectra of BSA-Cu NCs, CTC and BSA-Cu NCs + CTC were investigated. In the absorption spectrum of BSA-Cu NCs, there was an obvious absorption peak at 275 nm, which was caused by Trp, Tyr and Phe amino acid residues in the internal structure of BSA. After the addition of CTC, the absorption spectrum of BSA-Cu NCs showed the characteristic absorption peak of CTC and the wavelength was unchanged, and the intensity of the absorption peak was enhanced, indicating that CTC was combined and sensitized by BSA [26]. This was because CTC entered the three-dimensional amino acid hydrophobic cavity in the internal structure of BSA and combined with it without destroying its structure, and the fluorescence emission of CTC was enhanced in the hydrophobic cavity environment [45,46]. In addition, there was no surface plasmon band of copper nanoparticles in the wavelength range of 500–600 nm, which further indicated that BSA-Cu NCs did not aggregate to form large-sized particles [47], and the addition of CTC caused the fluorescence quenching of BSA-Cu NCs was not due to ACQ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference of BSA-Cu NCs nanoprobe detection for CTC
ce:section.ce:para: There were many interfering impurities in the actual sample, which had a certain influence on the accuracy of the detection results of the fluorescent probe. In order to explore the specific detection of BSA-Cu NCs for CTC, different types of antibiotics (Kanamycin sulfate, Thiamphenicol, Chloramphenicol, Pefloxacin, Ofloxacin, Doxycycline, Oxytetracycline and Tetracycline, C=5 μM) were detected under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 6a, BSA-Cu NCs did not respond to kanamycin sulfate, thiamphenicol, chloramphenicol, pefloxacin and ofloxacin, and the fluorescence color remained red under 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 6b). Due to the similar molecular structure, BSA-Cu NCs had a weak response to doxycycline, oxytetracycline and tetracycline, but the sensitivity was not as good as CTC, and the fluorescence color changed slightly under 365 nm UV lamp, which proved that BSA-Cu NCs had high selectivity for the detection of CTC in various antibiotics.

In addition, as shown in Fig. 6c, the effected of amino acids (Glu, GSH, Gly, Cys, AA), cations (Na+, Cu2+, Ag+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, K+) and anions (I-, Br-, Cl-, F-, ClO-, NO3 -, SO4 2-, CO3 2-, OH-) on CTC detection were investigated. When the concentration of the interference and CTC was 5 μM, the fluorescence intensity ratio of I535/I635 did not change significantly, and the fluorescence color under 365 nm UV lamp did not change obviously (Fig. 6d), which fully demonstrated that BSA-Cu NCs had strong selectivity for detection of CTC. Moreover, the fluorescence colors under the 365 nm UV lamp were red and green before and after the addition of CTC in the presence of all the interfering substances, respectively, which proved that BSA-Cu NCs had strong anti-interference ability for the detection of CTC, an effective basis for the detection of CTC in actual samples was provided.

In order to evaluate the detection results of CTC by BSA-Cu NCs, four actual samples of honey, milk, lake water and BSA were selected for testing. Under extreme pH conditions, tetracycline antibiotics were degraded by isomerization and dehydration with strong acids and strong bases, and tend to chelated with metal ions and matrix proteins to form complexes, which may affected the detection results of actual samples [48]. However, in the detection of actual samples, the protein in the milk sample was removed by adding trichloroacetic acid, and the honey was diluted at a large concentration. Therefore, the protein content in the actual sample was very small, which was not enough to chelate with CTC to form a complex. In addition, the detection of CTC was carried out in a deionized water environment with pH of 7, and there was no strong acid and alkali to degrade CTC, so the structural of CTC will not changed in the actual samples. The above conclusions were confirmed by measuring the UV–vis absorption spectra of CTC in water and in the actual samples, respectively. As shown in Fig. S1 (see Supplementary Information), the characteristic absorption peak of CTC in the actual samples did not changed, which proved that the structure of CTC did not changed. The determination results were shown in Table 2. The recovery was between 90.20% and 108.07%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was between 0.10% and 2.33%, indicating that BSA-Cu NCs had good repeatability and accuracy in the detection of CTC in actual samples, which had certain reference value for the detection of CTC in food and actual environment. Furthermore, in order to obtain the effect of doxycycline on the detection of CTC in the actual samples, the detection results of different concentrations of CTC in actual samples in the presence of 1 μM doxycycline were analyzed and listed in Table S1. The results indicating that the presence of doxycycline had little effect on the reproducibility and accuracy of BSA-Cu NCs in the detection of CTC, and the nanoprobe had good selectivity for CTC detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual real-time detection of CTC using fluorescent film and smartphone APP
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 7, in order to improve the operability, PVA fluorescent films based on BSA-Cu NCs were prepared. The fluorescent film showed the characteristic red fluorescence emission of BSA-Cu NCs under 365 nm UV lamp. After adding different concentrations of CTC solution, the fluorescence color change of the fluorescent film was consistent with the fluorescence color change trend of BSA-Cu NCs probe titration process. The fluorescent color photos of the fluorescent films at different concentrations of CTC were taken by a smartphone, and the chromaticity analysis was performed using the color recognition APP to obtain the chromaticity ratios of green (G) and red (R). There was a functional relationship between G/R and CTC concentration in the range of 0–14 μM, so that the visual detection and semi-quantitative analysis of CTC by fluorescent film was realized. This strategy combined visual detection with smartphone devices perfectly, which reflected the advantages of fast, low cost, safety and real-time monitoring of the proposed method.

A simple, safe and fast multicolor fluorescence nanoprobe for sensitive and efficient visual detection of CTC was constructed by one-step synthesis of red fluorescent emission BSA-Cu NCs. The red fluorescence emission of BSA-Cu NCs was quenched due to the internal filtration effect of CTC. However, CTC could enter the three-dimensional hydrophobic cavity structure in BSA, and sensitized by BSA, which enhanced the characteristic green fluorescence emission of CTC. With the increase of CTC concentration, the fluorescence color gradually changed from red to green under 365 nm UV lamp. In addition, the combination of PVA fluorescent film and smartphone APP formed an intelligent strategy for accurate, rapid and real-time detection of CTC, and the proposed method can also be extended to the detection of water and food safety.

Ning Bi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Ya-Hua Xi: Investigation, Formal analysis, Software. Mei-Hua Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Supervision. Jun Xu: Formal analysis, Resources. Jian Gou: Investigation, Data curation, Software. Yong-xin Li: Visualization, Investigation. Li-Na Zhang: Visualization, Investigation. Lei Jia: Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52072113, No. U1904168), the Science Fund for Excellent Young Scholars of Henan Province (222300420047) and the Program for Science & Technology Innovation Talents in Universities of Henan Province (No. 20HASTIT015).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2023.113404.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
